pmos

UX Design Frameworks and Laws — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · Cross-Functional Skills. A product manager's (PM's) working map of the named rules designers reach for — Jakob Nielsen's ten usability heuristics, the psychology "laws" (Fitts's, Hick's, Jakob's, Miller's), and the Gestalt principles of perception — plus the part most lists skip: when each one actually applies, and where they get misused. It draws the key distinction between a heuristic (an expert rule of thumb) and a law (a principle grounded in psychology research), walks each major one in plain language with the design move it implies, shows how teams apply them through heuristic evaluation, and flags the limits (the famous "7±2" rule is largely a misreading). Written for any PM. Every term is defined the first time it appears, and every acronym is spelled out on first use. The goal is not to turn you into a designer — it is to let you recognize which principle a design choice rests on, and push back when a "law" is being cited where it does not fit. Source provenance: curation-consensus across Jon Yablonski's Laws of UX, the Nielsen Norman Group, the UX Design Institute, and the Wikipedia psychology corpus. 15 verified sources.

What this is (one paragraph)

UX (user experience) design has accumulated a set of named principles that practitioners cite the way engineers cite design patterns — shared shorthand for "here's why this works." The most popular collection is product designer Jon Yablonski's Laws of UX, described on its site as "a collection of best practices that designers can consider when building user interfaces," spanning heuristics, Gestalt principles, cognitive biases, and general principles. Crucially, Yablonski did not invent these — each traces back to existing psychology or usability research; his contribution was organizing them into one accessible reference. The UX Design Institute counts roughly 21 such laws and notes "they help us to understand how people perceive and interact with our products — allowing us to make better design choices." This primer selects the handful a PM actually hears in reviews and frames when each applies.

Why this matters for a PM

When a designer says "that violates Hick's law" or "we should follow Jakob's law here," a PM who knows the vocabulary can engage with the reasoning instead of deferring to it. These principles also turn vague design opinions into checkable claims — "this is hard to scan" becomes a specific Gestalt or heuristic issue you can point at. The Nielsen Norman Group (a leading UX research firm) frames its ten heuristics as broad rules of thumb that have stayed relevant since 1994, useful for spotting where users will get lost or surprised. And there is a method behind them, not just vibes: as one reference notes, Nielsen deliberately sought "a compact list of the most important and most general principles, so that it would be manageable, memorable, and mature enough to last a long time." Knowing them lets a PM tell a grounded critique from a personal preference.

Heuristics vs. laws: what's the difference?

The terms get used loosely, but the distinction matters. A heuristic is an experience-based rule of thumb — a guideline a skilled person applies with judgment, not a guarantee; Nielsen's ten are heuristics. A law, in this field, is a principle grounded in psychology or human-factors research, often with a measurable effect — Fitts's law and Hick's law come from mid-20th-century experiments on human movement and decision-making. The UX Design Institute sorts the whole body into categories — heuristics, Gestalt principles, cognitive biases, and general principles — precisely because they have different kinds of authority. The practical upshot for a PM: a "law" carries research weight but still has conditions where it applies, while a "heuristic" is explicitly a judgment call. Treating either as an unbreakable rule is the most common way they get misused.

Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics

The ten usability heuristics are the canonical checklist, published by Jakob Nielsen in 1994 and still the default lens for spotting interface problems. They include visibility of system status (the first and most cited: "The design should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within a reasonable amount of time"), user control and freedom, consistency and standards, error prevention, and recognition rather than recall. Nielsen, who co-founded the Nielsen Norman Group, paired them with a lightweight testing philosophy — he argued "elaborate usability tests are a waste of resources" because a handful of users surface most flaws. For a PM, the ten are a fast vocabulary for naming what's wrong: a checkout that gives no confirmation is a visibility-of-system-status failure, full stop.

Fitts's law — size and distance of targets

Fitts's law is one of the oldest and most reliable, from psychologist Paul Fitts in the 1950s. It predicts that, per the research, "the time required to rapidly move to a target area is a function of the ratio between the distance to the target and the width of the target" — in plain terms, big, close things are fast to hit; small, far things are slow and error-prone. The design move, as Yablonski frames it, is to make tap targets large and reachable, since "the time to acquire a target is a function of the distance to and size of the target," which matters most on touch screens where fingers are imprecise. This is why primary buttons are big, why mobile menus sit at the bottom within thumb reach, and why a tiny "X" in the far corner frustrates people. For a PM, Fitts's law is the rule behind "make the main action obvious and easy to hit."

Hick's law — number of choices

Hick's law addresses decision time. The research finding is that "increasing the number of choices will increase the decision time logarithmically" — more options mean slower decisions, though sub-linearly, because people subdivide choices rather than scanning every one. The design implication, in Yablonski's framing, is to simplify: "The time it takes to make a decision increases with the number and complexity of choices," so reduce options and break complex tasks into smaller steps. This is the principle behind progressive onboarding, curated default plans, and hiding advanced settings. The PM caution: Hick's law is about decision time, not whether choice is good — sometimes more options is the right product call, and the law just tells you the cost.

Jakob's law — the power of convention

Jakob's law (named for Nielsen) is about familiarity. Per the law, "Users prefer your site to work the same way as all the other sites they already know" — and the reason is mental models: "by leveraging existing mental models, we can create superior user experiences in which the users can focus on their tasks rather than on learning new models." A mental model is the internal expectation a user brings about how something should work, built up from every other product they've used. This is why a shopping cart icon belongs top-right, why a logo links home, and why reinventing a standard control usually backfires. For a PM, Jakob's law is the strongest argument against novel interactions "for differentiation" — novelty has a real learning cost, and convention is usually the safer default unless the payoff is large.

Miller's law — and the "7±2" myth

Miller's law is the most misquoted in the field. It comes from George Miller's 1956 paper finding that short-term memory holds roughly seven "chunks" of information — but later research revised that down toward four chunks, and Miller himself said "there is nothing 'magical' about the number seven." The popular claim that menus should have 7±2 items is a misreading: Miller studied memory recall, not interface design, and never prescribed a limit on visible options (which users read, not memorize). The legitimate design takeaway, per Yablonski, is chunking — "Organize content into smaller chunks to help users process, understand, and memorize easily" — like grouping a phone number or breaking a long form into sections. For a PM, this is the cautionary tale: a real research finding got flattened into a fake rule, so "Miller's law says max seven" deserves a polite challenge.

Gestalt principles — how the eye groups things

The Gestalt principles come from early-20th-century Gestalt psychology, whose core claim is that people perceive organized wholes, not isolated parts — "The whole is something else than the sum of its parts." The principles of grouping — proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, and common region — predict how the eye bundles elements: "Objects or shapes that are close to one another appear to form groups," even when they look different. In practice these are the rules behind visual hierarchy: items placed close together read as related (proximity), shared color or shape implies a set (similarity), and a border or shaded box binds a group (common region). For a PM, Gestalt is the answer to "why does this screen feel cluttered or confusing" — usually the spacing and grouping are fighting the content's actual structure.

Applying them: heuristic evaluation

The heuristics become actionable through heuristic evaluation — a usability inspection method where, typically, three to five evaluators independently inspect an interface against a recognized set of principles (usually Nielsen's ten), flag each violation, and rate its severity. It is popular because it is fast and cheap: it finds many major problems "without running a full user test," as a quick audit before or instead of recruiting real users. The method's value comes from the list being "manageable, memorable" enough to apply consistently. The important nuance — well established in the research — is that heuristic evaluation and real usability testing find different problems: experts catch principle-level issues, real users reveal context-specific ones, so the two are complements, not substitutes. A PM should expect both, not treat a heuristic pass as proof the design works.

When each one applies

The skill is matching the principle to the problem rather than reciting all of them. A motor question — can people quickly hit the thing they want? — is Fitts's law territory (target size and placement). A decision question — are we slowing people down with too many options? — is Hick's law. A familiarity question — are we breaking an expectation users carry from everywhere else? — is Jakob's law. A perception question — does the layout group things the way the content actually relates? — is Gestalt. And a broad "is this usable at all" sweep is Nielsen's heuristics. Naming the right category first is what turns these from trivia into a working diagnostic — and it keeps a review focused on one kind of problem at a time.

Limits and misuse

These principles are guidelines, not physics, and the honest framing is that each has boundaries. The clearest cautionary case is Miller's "7±2," a genuine memory finding bent into a fake interface rule — a reminder that a cited "law" can be a misreading. Even solid laws have conditions: Hick's law describes decision time but says nothing about whether a choice is worth offering, and Jakob's law favors convention but would freeze all innovation if followed absolutely. The body of laws is best held as a checklist of considerations, not commandments — Yablonski himself presents them as "best practices that designers can consider," with the operative word being consider. For a PM, the most useful move is to ask, when a law is cited, "does the condition behind it actually hold here?" — that question separates real application from cargo-cult citation.

Concrete next actions you can take this week

You can start using these immediately. First, run an informal heuristic evaluation on one core flow against Nielsen's ten — just walk it and note where each principle is violated. Second, take one screen that "feels off" and diagnose it with Gestalt grouping: are related things actually close together, and unrelated things apart? Third, the next time a designer or stakeholder cites a "law," ask whether the condition behind it holds — especially if it's "7±2." Fourth, before approving a novel interaction, weigh it against Jakob's law — is the differentiation worth the learning cost? These habits turn a list of names into a daily review instinct.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

These laws are one layer of a larger UX toolkit, and each connects outward to a neighboring topic worth a separate look. A few of the most useful adjacencies are listed below as pointers, not lessons — each is its own primer-sized subject. Follow whichever maps to the question in front of you right now.

  • The five elements of UX design — Garrett's layered model; these laws mostly live on its structure, skeleton, and surface planes.
  • Usability testing — the complement to heuristic evaluation: watching real users instead of inspecting against rules.
  • Cognitive biases in product — the other half of the Laws of UX (anchoring, the peak-end rule) that shape behavior, not just perception.
  • Accessibility — many of these principles (Fitts's targets, clear status, convention) are also accessibility requirements, not just nice-to-haves.
  • Information architecture — how content is structured before these perception-level rules are applied to a screen.

References

  1. lawsofux.com ·cited· (T1)

    Jon Yablonski's Laws of UX is the field's best-known curation of psychology-based design principles — "a collection of best practices that designers can consider when building user interfaces" — grouping heuristics, Gestalt principles, cognitive biases, and general principles in one reference.

  2. uxdesigninstitute.com ·cited· (T1)

    The UX Design Institute frames the ~21 Laws of UX as psychology-rooted principles in four categories (heuristics, Gestalt, cognitive biases, general); "they help us to understand how people perceive and interact with our products — allowing us to make better design choices."

  3. nngroup.com ·cited· (T1)

    Jakob Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics (1994) are the most-used rules of thumb in UX — including visibility of system status, user control, and error prevention; the first reads "The design should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within a reasonable amount of time."

  4. lawsofux.com ·cited· (T1)

    In design terms Fitts's law says make tap targets big and reachable, because "the time to acquire a target is a function of the distance to and size of the target" — especially on touch screens where finger precision is limited.

  5. lawsofux.com ·cited· (T1)

    The design move from Hick's law is to simplify: "The time it takes to make a decision increases with the number and complexity of choices," so reduce options and break complex tasks into smaller steps.

  6. lawsofux.com ·cited· (T1)

    Jakob's law says favor convention: "Users prefer your site to work the same way as all the other sites they already know," and "by leveraging existing mental models, we can create superior user experiences in which the users can focus on their tasks rather than on learning new models."

  7. lawsofux.com ·cited· (T1)

    The legitimate design reading of Miller's law is chunking, not a hard cap: "Organize content into smaller chunks to help users process, understand, and memorize easily."

  8. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Jakob Nielsen, co-founder of the Nielsen Norman Group, authored the 10 heuristics and champions lightweight testing; he argued "elaborate usability tests are a waste of resources" because as few as five users surface most critical flaws.

  9. thedecisionlab.com ·cited· (T2)

    Nielsen built the list to be usable: he sought "a compact list of the most important and most general principles, so that it would be manageable, memorable, and mature enough to last a long time" — which is why heuristic evaluation against them is the default quick audit.

  10. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Fitts's law is the research finding behind target sizing: "the time required to rapidly move to a target area is a function of the ratio between the distance to the target and the width of the target" — far, small targets are slow and error-prone.

  11. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Hick's law quantifies choice overload: "increasing the number of choices will increase the decision time logarithmically" — reaction time rises with the number and complexity of options, though sub-linearly because people subdivide choices.

  12. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    George Miller's 1956 paper on working-memory limits is widely misused: later research puts capacity nearer four chunks, and "there is nothing 'magical' about the number seven" — it never prescribed a limit on menu items or interface options.

  13. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Gestalt psychology holds that people perceive organized wholes, not isolated parts — "The whole is something else than the sum of its parts" — which is why layout cues like proximity and similarity drive how an interface reads.

  14. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    The Gestalt grouping principles (proximity, similarity, closure, continuity, common region) predict perception: "Objects or shapes that are close to one another appear to form groups" even when they look quite different — the basis of visual hierarchy and spacing.

  15. en.wikipedia.org ·cited· (T2)

    Heuristic evaluation is the method that makes the heuristics actionable — an expert inspection, typically 3–5 reviewers checking an interface against recognized principles to find major usability problems quickly and cheaply, without a full user test.
